Keeping later work on that service/model/migration seam avoids scattering auth changes into JWT/session helpers or reinterpreting global startup Base.metadata.create_all compatibility bootstrap as request-path auth DDL.

decisions.decision: How password-reset lifecycle invariants and Alembic revision ids are formalized for M016/S01
decisions.choice: Enforce the single-active-password-reset-token invariant with a partial unique index on password_reset_tokens.user_id, and keep password-reset Alembic revision IDs at 32 characters or fewer.
decisions.rationale: The runtime service already supersedes prior tokens, but without a DB constraint the lifecycle contract remains soft and duplicate active rows can still be inserted. Alembic in this project also uses the default alembic_version.version_num width, so overlong revision IDs fail fresh migration state updates before the auth schema reaches head. Hardening both at the schema layer makes token lifecycle health and migration state mechanically checkable.

decisions.decision: How M016/S01 should prove password-reset lifecycle behavior without creating a new umbrella auth suite
decisions.choice: Keep the existing repo-root auth gate (`backend/tests/integration/test_auth_login_api.py`) as the compatibility proof surface and pair it with the dedicated reset lifecycle suite (`backend/tests/integration/test_password_reset_api.py`) for detailed token-state assertions.
decisions.rationale: The auth login suite is already the repo-root compatibility gate for hashed-password-first login behavior and request-path contract checks, while the dedicated password-reset suite already owns row-level lifecycle assertions like supersession, expiry, and single-active-token enforcement. Extending those two focused seams keeps proof narrow, mechanically re-runnable, and avoids introducing a third broader entry point that would duplicate coverage and make future auth hardening harder to localize.

decisions.decision: M016/S02 backend/frontend shared error-contract seam
decisions.choice: Use `common.api.response.error_response` + `common.api.server_error.build_server_error` as the backend outward error seam, and `web/src/lib/api/client.ts` `normalizeApiErrorPayload`/`ApiRequestError` as the frontend normalization seam; treat prompt templates as the closest existing model, while presentations string-detail branches, auth dependency `HTTPException` strings, and `getSegmentAudioBlobUrl()` manual parsing are the drift points to collapse in T02.
decisions.rationale: The prompt-template routes already prove the narrowest reusable pattern: domain/not-found 4xx carry `{error,message}` and infra 5xx go through the shared Result-style envelope with trace ids. Locking that seam avoids a broad FastAPI-wide exception rewrite and tells downstream work exactly where the remaining outward drift still leaks through.

decisions.decision: How to normalize focused backend 4xx error envelopes in M016/S02
decisions.choice: Use route-local `JSONResponse(error_response(...))` for the audited prompt-template and presentation 4xx surfaces, while keeping auth dependency failures on structured `HTTPException.detail={error,message}` because dependencies cannot safely short-circuit with response returns.
decisions.rationale: FastAPI's shared HTTPException handler currently stringifies dict detail into the top-level `error/message` fields, so relying on bare route-level HTTPException keeps stable codes hidden under `detail` and forces clients to guess. Returning `JSONResponse(error_response(...))` from the focused route families yields the same top-level envelope as existing 5xx helpers without attempting a risky global exception rewrite. Auth dependencies still need to raise, so the smallest safe contract is to make their detail payload structured and let the frontend normalizer read it.

decisions.decision: How protected-route role/admin dependency failures should stay on the unified M016/S02 error seam
decisions.choice: Make `get_current_admin_user` and `require_role(...)` reuse `_raise_auth_http_error(...)` so dependency-level 403 responses keep `detail={error,message}` instead of raw-string `HTTPException.detail`.
decisions.rationale: Route-local 4xx responses now use `JSONResponse(error_response(...))`, but dependency-based permission failures still must raise. Reusing the structured auth helper keeps frontend `ApiRequestError` normalization on one stable path across user, admin, and role-guard failures without attempting a risky global FastAPI exception rewrite.

decisions.decision: M016/S03 admin security baseline should prioritize one fix-first route/log group
decisions.choice: Treat the legacy admin route families still using get_current_user (admin.py, analytics.py, release_verification.py, system_logs.py, training_records.py) plus the shared logging sinks in common.monitoring.logger and latency_tracker as the first repair group, while using admin.users as the positive-control proof seam.
decisions.rationale: These surfaces combine the highest certainty with the highest blast radius: they are already mounted under /admin yet lack an admin-only role guard, or they sit on shared logging boundaries that can leak token/password/cookie/email fields across many callers. Starting from them keeps S03 focused on real risk reduction instead of broad backend-wide scanning.

decisions.decision: Where admin-route RBAC and sensitive-log redaction are enforced for the M016/S03 fix-first surfaces
decisions.choice: Make fix-first admin routers declare Depends(get_current_admin_user) in their own modules and centralize token/password/cookie/email redaction in common.monitoring.logger, with auth routes emitting structured fields instead of stringified credential-path exceptions.
decisions.rationale: Router-local admin dependencies keep the permission boundary visible and safe even when a router is mounted outside main.py, while a shared logger sanitizer covers auth, latency, and future structured sinks uniformly without relying on fragile call-site masking.

decisions.decision: How M016/S03 admin security baseline proof maps inventory watch entries to runtime tests
decisions.choice: Keep the repaired five isolated-router families on explicit `ADMIN_SECURITY_BASELINE_WATCH_ROUTE_PROOFS` coverage and track already-correct admin families separately through `ADMIN_PERMISSION_POSITIVE_CONTROL` instead of forcing every inventory `priority="watch"` entry into the same runtime deny test.
decisions.rationale: The S03 inventory uses `priority="watch"` for two different reasons: some route families were repaired and need isolated-router RBAC proof, while other admin families are already correctly guarded and only need ongoing positive-control tracking. Splitting those cases keeps the baseline honest, prevents over-broad runtime assertions, and makes future admin-security audits update the inventory deliberately rather than widening proof scope accidentally.

decisions.decision: Session lifecycle stale-writer convergence strategy
decisions.choice: Use optimistic compare-and-swap on PracticeSession.status and converge stale non-terminal writers to the already-persisted terminal state instead of introducing row locks.
decisions.rationale: The focused lifecycle proof runs on SQLite-backed tests where row-lock behavior is not a reliable or easily provable contract. A status-guarded UPDATE makes the race observable at write time, preserves existing sales/presentation terminal differences plus report/replay semantics, and lets stale pause/resume attempts become explicit no-op convergence when end already won.

decisions.decision: Authoritative proof seam for session lifecycle concurrency contract
decisions.choice: Keep the lifecycle concurrency contract in the focused unit/integration lifecycle tests and use the existing repo-root pytest command as the long-term regression entrypoint; isolate presentation terminal-split assertions from unrelated coach-service duration math when the proof target is lifecycle terminal semantics.
decisions.rationale: The slice goal is to keep stale pause/resume/end races mechanically provable on the same authority seam that future slices will reuse. Keeping the contract next to the focused lifecycle proofs avoids a second documentation-only source of truth, while stubbing unrelated presentation coach duration behavior prevents a known timezone-mismatch issue from obscuring whether sales still settles to `scoring` and presentation to `completed`.

decisions.decision: How to treat the remaining complexity inside use-practice-websocket during M017/S02
decisions.choice: Keep use-practice-websocket as the transport/outbound orchestrator and target future refactors at the reconnect/backpressure/interrupt seam instead of splitting again by file size; keep inbound protocol projection in websocket/message-handlers.
decisions.rationale: Reading the live hook and focused tests showed the real coordination burden is shared local runtime cleanup and outbound pacing across websocket state, audio queues, reconnect retry budget, binary negotiation, and interrupt pre-cleanup. Inbound status/reconnected/interrupted/backpressure application is already isolated in message-handlers, so another size-driven split would move code without reducing the true seam.

decisions.decision: How pending outbound messages behave across practice websocket reconnects and interrupt cleanup
decisions.choice: Treat reconnect as a fresh transport epoch: only initial connect may replay queued outbound messages, while reconnect/failed phases drop queued messages and interrupt clears both queued outbound messages and local backpressure/slow-state flags.
decisions.rationale: The focused websocket proofs showed two stale-state hazards in the live hook: an interrupt fired against a dead socket was replayed on the next connection, and interrupting a buffered/backpressured turn left learner-visible slow-state flags behind even though the local backlog had been discarded. Narrowing pending replay to the initial handshake and making interrupt own local queue/backpressure cleanup keeps reconnect, backpressure, and interrupt semantics on one truthful orchestrator seam without adding a second state machine.

decisions.decision: What should be the authoritative discovery seam for presentation upload/resource race work in M017/S03
decisions.choice: Use the code-adjacent `PRESENTATION_RESOURCE_RACE_INVENTORY` in `backend/src/presentation_coach/api/presentations.py` plus focused presentation contract/integration proofs as the authority, and prioritize reproducing concurrent in-place replace before adding broad lock/distributed-lock work.
decisions.rationale: The live code now shows three distinct mutation surfaces: new uploads are mostly isolated by fresh storage keys, delete has a confirmed route-guard gap that current focused proof can observe directly, and in-place replace remains the highest-value race candidate because it mutates the stable `presentation_id` authority after only a read-then-write preflight. Locking the discovery seam next to the runtime authority keeps later agents from re-auditing the same routes from scratch and avoids premature distributed-lock rollout before the concurrent replace race is actually reproduced.

decisions.decision: What harness should future agents use to reproduce presentation replace races truthfully
decisions.choice: Use a per-request `get_db` override backed by a shared file-backed SQLite engine for concurrent presentation replace proofs; do not use the shared `backend/tests/conftest.py::async_client` session for race reproduction.
decisions.rationale: The focused proof showed the shared async_client fixture reuses one AsyncSession across requests and can fail for shared-session reasons before it says anything truthful about route-level concurrency. Fresh-session requests on one shared database reproduce the real replace race and keep future concurrency work grounded in the live authority seam.
